inputObservationForm.learnerResponse: 2 x 2 x 3처럼 소수만 남겨서 다시 써야 해. 2가 하나 더 필요해.
inputObservationForm.tutorNote: 학습자가 composite factor를 쓰지 말고 누락된 2를 다시 포함해야 한다고 설명함.

latestReplayResult.name: Active S5A transfer log with Korean text-only observation

messages.speaker: tutor
messages.text: 약수를 하나씩 다 쓰기 전에, 이미 한 소인수분해에서 무엇을 바로 가져올 수 있을까?

messages.speaker: learner
messages.text: 약수의 개수를 보는 문제니까 약수를 일일이 쓰지 말고 소인수분해 결과부터 써야 해.

turnResults.operatorNote: 튜터가 factorization reuse 발화만 남기고 체크박스는 비워 두었다.

nextRecommendedSession.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
nextRecommendedSession.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.

steps.title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
steps.openingLine: You do not need to restart from zero here; let's reuse what the factorization already tells us.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
steps.smallHint: Write the prime factorization first, then use it instead of listing divisors one by one.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner starts the divisor-count task from the factorization rather than restarting blindly.

latestRecommendations.targetSkillTitle: Use prime factorization to reason about divisor count
latestRecommendations.summary: Learner restarts from scratch instead of using factorization to reason about divisor count.

recommendedActivities.title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
recommendedActivities.goal: Stop the learner from restarting divisor counting from scratch.
recommendedActivities.firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
recommendedActivities.operatorFocus: Keep the learner from listing every divisor one by one.
watchFor: Learner abandons the factorization they already built.
Learner starts listing divisors without explaining what the task is asking for.
recommendedActivities.exampleTutorMove: Ask what information the completed factorization already gives before allowing any divisor list.

steps.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
steps.openingLine: Your split-work is close; now let's turn it into one clean final factorization.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
steps.smallHint: Keep only prime factors in the last line, and make sure none were dropped.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner writes one complete prime factorization with no missing or composite factor.
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: The final line should keep 2, 2, and 3 because all of them are prime and none can be dropped.

steps.title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
steps.openingLine: You do not need to restart from zero here; let's reuse what the factorization already tells us.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
steps.smallHint: Write the prime factorization first, then use it instead of listing divisors one by one.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner starts the divisor-count task from the factorization rather than restarting blindly.
watchFor: Learner ignores an already completed factorization and restarts from scratch.
Learner lists divisors one by one without naming what the task asks for.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Before listing anything, tell me what the factorization already gives you for this question.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: I need the number of divisors, so I should use the factorization instead of listing every divisor.

recommendedActivitySequence.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
recommendedActivitySequence.goal: Stabilize the move from split-work to a complete final prime factorization.
recommendedActivitySequence.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
recommendedActivitySequence.operatorFocus: Push for one clean final line, not more splitting work.
watchFor: Learner drops a repeated factor while rewriting.
Learner leaves a composite factor in the final line.
recommendedActivitySequence.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and ask the learner to rebuild only the final prime-factor line.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write the learner's clean final prime-factor line.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner kept all repeated factors and removed composite factors.

fields.label: Only prime factors
fields.prompt: Did the learner write the final line using only prime factors?
fields.trueMeans: The learner removed any composite factor from the final line.

fields.label: Keeps all factors
fields.prompt: Did the learner keep every needed factor, including repeats?
fields.trueMeans: The learner did not drop a repeated or needed factor.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write how the learner started the divisor-count transfer task.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner reused the factorization or restarted from scratch.

fields.label: Starts from factorization
fields.prompt: Did the learner begin from the existing prime factorization?
fields.trueMeans: The learner reused the factorization instead of opening a new search.

fields.label: Avoids blind listing
fields.prompt: Did the learner avoid listing divisors one by one without a reason?
fields.trueMeans: The learner stayed with structure-based reasoning.

steps.title: Entry Bridge With 24
steps.openingLine: Let's just find one easy way into the problem instead of solving everything at once.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: What is the easiest divisor you can test first?
steps.smallHint: If the number is even, checking 2 is a calm first step.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner starts with 2 for 24 and explains that 24 is even.
watchFor: Learner stalls because they think they need the whole tree immediately.
Learner notices 24 is even but still does not choose 2.
steps.exampleTutorMove: You do not need the whole answer yet. What does the word 'even' let you try first?
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: 24 is even, so trying 2 first is the easiest entry.

steps.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
steps.openingLine: Your split-work is close; now let's turn it into one clean final factorization.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
steps.smallHint: Keep only prime factors in the last line, and make sure none were dropped.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner writes one complete prime factorization with no missing or composite factor.
watchFor: Learner keeps a composite factor like 6 in the final line.
Learner rewrites with only prime factors but drops one repeated factor.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and rebuild the last line only with prime factors that still remain.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: The final line should keep 2, 2, and 3 because all of them are prime and none can be dropped.

blockedByPrerequisites.whyBlocked: Prerequisite skill U1-S2 does not yet have learner evidence, so progress on U1-S3 should stay conservative.

recommendedActivitySequence.title: Entry Bridge With 24
recommendedActivitySequence.goal: Rebuild the habit of starting with an easy divisor pair instead of freezing.
recommendedActivitySequence.firstTutorQuestion: What is the easiest divisor you can test first?
recommendedActivitySequence.operatorFocus: Reward a calm starting move instead of speed.
watchFor: Learner freezes because they think they must finish the whole factorization at once.
Learner chooses a hard divisor first even after noticing the number is even.
recommendedActivitySequence.exampleTutorMove: If the learner hesitates, point back to the word 'even' and ask what divisor that suggests.

recommendedActivitySequence.title: Rewrite the Final Factorization Cleanly
recommendedActivitySequence.goal: Stabilize the move from split-work to a complete final prime factorization.
recommendedActivitySequence.firstTutorQuestion: Which factors should still remain in the final line?
recommendedActivitySequence.operatorFocus: Push for one clean final line, not more splitting work.
watchFor: Learner drops a repeated factor while rewriting.
Learner leaves a composite factor in the final line.
recommendedActivitySequence.exampleTutorMove: Cover the scratch work and ask the learner to rebuild only the final prime-factor line.

latestRecommendations.targetSkillTitle: Use prime factorization to reason about divisor count
latestRecommendations.summary: Learner restarts from scratch instead of using factorization to reason about divisor count.

recommendedActivities.title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
recommendedActivities.goal: Stop the learner from restarting divisor counting from scratch.
recommendedActivities.firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
recommendedActivities.operatorFocus: Keep the learner from listing every divisor one by one.
watchFor: Learner abandons the factorization they already built.
Learner starts listing divisors without explaining what the task is asking for.
recommendedActivities.exampleTutorMove: Ask what information the completed factorization already gives before allowing any divisor list.

steps.title: Reuse Factorization for Divisor Count
steps.openingLine: You do not need to restart from zero here; let's reuse what the factorization already tells us.
steps.firstTutorQuestion: In this problem, do you need the factorization itself or the number of divisors?
steps.smallHint: Write the prime factorization first, then use it instead of listing divisors one by one.
steps.goodStoppingPoint: Learner starts the divisor-count task from the factorization rather than restarting blindly.
watchFor: Learner ignores an already completed factorization and restarts from scratch.
Learner lists divisors one by one without naming what the task asks for.
steps.exampleTutorMove: Before listing anything, tell me what the factorization already gives you for this question.
steps.exampleLearnerResponse: I need the number of divisors, so I should use the factorization instead of listing every divisor.

observationFormTemplate.learnerResponsePrompt: Write how the learner started the divisor-count transfer task.
observationFormTemplate.tutorNotePrompt: Note whether the learner reused the factorization or restarted from scratch.

fields.label: Starts from factorization
fields.prompt: Did the learner begin from the existing prime factorization?
fields.trueMeans: The learner reused the factorization instead of opening a new search.

fields.label: Avoids blind listing
fields.prompt: Did the learner avoid listing divisors one by one without a reason?
fields.trueMeans: The learner stayed with structure-based reasoning.
